Virupaksha Temple.name: Virupaksha Temple
Virupaksha Temple.history: The Virupaksha Temple is one of the oldest and most sacred temples in Hampi, dedicated to Lord Shiva in his form as Virupaksha (Pampapati). Built in the 7th century CE, it was the principal royal temple of the Vijayanagara Empire and remains an active place of worship to this day — a remarkable testament to 1,300 years of uninterrupted Hindu tradition. The towering 50-metre gopuram (gateway tower) was added in the 15th century and is adorned with intricate sculptural panels. The temple complex includes a pillared hall, sacred tank, and subsidiary shrines. It served as the center of Hampi's religious and cultural life during the empire's golden age (14th–16th century CE).
Virupaksha Temple.best_time: Early morning or evening for golden-hour photography

Vittala Temple.name: Vittala Temple
Vittala Temple.history: The Vittala Temple complex is widely regarded as the pinnacle of Vijayanagara art and architecture, constructed primarily during the reign of Emperor Krishnadevaraya (1509–1529 CE). Dedicated to Lord Vittala (a form of Vishnu), the temple is most famous for its iconic Stone Chariot — a miniature shrine carved from a single granite block, featuring wheels that once actually rotated. The main hall boasts 56 musical pillars (sapta-swara sthambhas) that emit distinct musical notes when struck, a feat of acoustic engineering still astonishing engineers today. The elaborate carvings depict horses, elephants, mythological scenes and celestial dancers, showcasing the empire's wealth and artistic sophistication at its zenith.
Vittala Temple.ticket_price: ₹40 (Indians) / ₹600 (Foreigners). Combo ticket with other monuments available.
Vittala Temple.best_time: Morning 8–11 AM to avoid afternoon heat

Lotus Mahal.name: Lotus Mahal
Lotus Mahal.history: The Lotus Mahal is a delicate two-storied pavilion in the Zenana Enclosure (royal women's quarter) of Hampi, built in the late 15th or early 16th century CE. Its name derives from the lotus-bud shape of its arched niches and the floral motifs that adorn every surface. The structure uniquely blends Indo-Islamic and South Indian architectural styles — pointed arches typical of Islamic design sit harmoniously alongside Hindu ornamentation. The building is believed to have served as a pleasure pavilion or council chamber for the royal women. Its remarkably preserved stucco work and elegant proportions make it one of Hampi's most photographed structures and an outstanding example of Vijayanagara secular architecture.
Lotus Mahal.best_time: Morning for soft light on the white stucco

Elephant Stables.name: Elephant Stables
Elephant Stables.history: The Elephant Stables (Gajashala) is a magnificent row of 11 interconnected chambers that once housed the royal war elephants of the Vijayanagara Empire. Built in the late 15th century, this grand structure stretches nearly 100 metres and combines Hindu and Islamic architectural elements — each chamber is topped by a different style of dome (ribbed, fluted, or hemispherical), creating a visually rhythmic facade. The central chamber, larger than the rest, may have served as the mahout's quarters or a ceremonial hall. The sheer scale reflects how highly prized war elephants were in medieval Indian military strategy. The stables are located within the Royal Enclosure, surrounded by watchtowers and guard chambers.
Elephant Stables.best_time: Afternoon for dramatic shadows on the domed roof

Hazara Rama Temple.name: Hazara Rama Temple
Hazara Rama Temple.history: The Hazara Rama Temple (meaning 'Temple of a Thousand Ramas') was the private royal chapel of the Vijayanagara kings, located within the Royal Enclosure. Dating to the early 15th century, it is dedicated to Lord Vishnu in his Rama avatar. The temple is celebrated for its extraordinarily detailed bas-relief friezes that wrap around the outer walls, narrating episodes from the Ramayana in sequential panels — a visual scripture carved in stone. The interior pillars feature polished black stone sculptures of remarkable refinement. Uniquely, the outer enclosure wall also carries extensive relief carvings depicting processions of elephants, horses, soldiers and dancing girls, providing vivid insight into court life during the Vijayanagara era.
Hazara Rama Temple.best_time: Morning for clear views of the bas-relief carvings

Achyutaraya Temple.name: Achyutaraya Temple
Achyutaraya Temple.history: The Achyutaraya Temple, also known as Tiruvengalanatha Temple, was built in 1534 CE during the reign of Emperor Achyuta Deva Raya, the last great Vijayanagara ruler before the empire's decline. Dedicated to Lord Vishnu as Tiruvengalanatha, it stands at the base of Matanga Hill between the Vittala and Virupaksha temples. Though partially ruined, the temple complex retains two magnificent gopurams and an extensive bazaar street lined with pillared mandapas — once a thriving marketplace. The temple's carving quality and the atmospheric decay among tall boulders give it a distinctive, slightly melancholic grandeur. It sees far fewer tourists than Vittala, offering a more contemplative experience.
Achyutaraya Temple.best_time: Sunset from nearby Matanga Hill offers a panoramic view

Matanga Hill.name: Matanga Hill
Matanga Hill.history: Matanga Hill is the highest point in Hampi, rising prominently above the boulder-strewn landscape and offering a 360-degree panorama of the entire ancient city — the Virupaksha Temple spire, the Tungabhadra River, the Vittala Temple complex, and the Royal Enclosure can all be seen from its summit. According to the Ramayana, the sage Matanga meditated here, making it a site of deep mythological significance. A small Shiva temple crowns the summit. The climb involves ascending hundreds of steps carved into the rock face and is most popular at sunrise, when the mist lifts over the ruins and the first light illuminates the boulder landscape in shades of gold and amber — an experience considered unmissable by visitors to Hampi.
Matanga Hill.timings: Sunrise to Sunset (best visited at dawn)

Underground Shiva Temple.name: Underground Shiva Temple
Underground Shiva Temple.history: The Underground Shiva Temple, formally known as the Prasanna Virupaksha Temple, is one of the most unusual and atmospheric monuments in Hampi. Built in the 14th century, the temple is partially submerged — its inner sanctum lies below ground level and floods with water during the monsoon season, leaving the Shivalinga partially submerged for months. This creates a hauntingly beautiful spectacle. The structure was deliberately built at a lower level, possibly to create a cave-like, intimate setting befitting Shiva's ascetic nature. The entrance corridor features slender carved pillars and leads down steps into the darkened sanctum. Frogs and lotus flowers often share the space with the Shivalinga during the monsoon — a uniquely symbolic coexistence.

Queen's Bath.name: Queen's Bath
Queen's Bath.history: The Queen's Bath is a luxurious royal bathing pavilion located within Hampi's Royal Enclosure, dating to the late 15th century. Despite its name, historians believe it may have been used by royalty of both genders for bathing ceremonies. The square structure surrounds a large open-air pool, with an ornate corridor featuring arched balconies overlooking the water — the queens and royal women could have observed ceremonies while remaining in purdah behind the decorated screens. The interior walls once bore elaborate stucco decorations, traces of which survive. Lotus-shaped fountains fed by a sophisticated aqueduct system once perfumed the bath with scented water. The building's fusion of Islamic arched balconies with Hindu decorative motifs is a hallmark of Vijayanagara's syncretic court culture.
Queen's Bath.best_time: Late afternoon when the interior is well-lit

Hampi Bazaar.name: Hampi Bazaar
Hampi Bazaar.history: Hampi Bazaar is the ancient marketplace that once stretched directly in front of the Virupaksha Temple, forming the bustling commercial heart of the Vijayanagara capital. During the empire's golden age (14th–16th century), this kilometre-long colonnaded street was one of the grandest bazaars in the known world, where merchants traded gems, silks, spices and horses. Contemporary accounts by Portuguese traveler Domingo Paes described the market as rivalling Lisbon in wealth and splendour. Today the stone-pillared mandapas (market halls) stand in atmospheric ruin on either side of the wide street, while the modern village of Hampi has grown up organically among the ancient stones. The bazaar street offers a living palimpsest of ancient grandeur and everyday village life — a unique experience in India.
Hampi Bazaar.timings: Open all day (best at golden hour)
Hampi Bazaar.best_time: Early morning before tourist crowds, or sunset
